news_summary: Innovative environmental efforts are advancing flood control through urban beavers, promoting sustainable product design, emphasizing the essential role of urban trees, and encouraging clothing repair to reduce fast fashion waste.
news_summary_fr: Des initiatives environnementales innovantes contribuent à améliorer la gestion des inondations grâce aux castors urbains, favorisent la conception de produits durables, mettent en avant le rôle essentiel des arbres en milieu urbain et encouragent la réparation des vêtements afin de réduire les déchets liés à la « fast fashion ».
news_summary_es: Las iniciativas medioambientales innovadoras están mejorando la gestión de las inundaciones gracias a los castores urbanos, fomentando el diseño sostenible de productos, destacando el papel esencial de los árboles urbanos y promoviendo la reparación de la ropa para reducir los residuos generados por la moda rápida.

articles.title: How west London beavers are tackling flooding
articles.summary: New beavers in west London have prevented flooding to Greenford station three years in a row.
articles.content:
A colony of beavers reintroduced to Ealing has helped stop a railway station from flooding, according to the project behind their return, which is also celebrating the birth of more kits.

The Ealing Beaver Project says Greenford station, downstream from the animals' wetland home at Paradise Fields, has not flooded for three years in a row since they moved in.

The beavers slow the flow of water during downpours and create wetlands that hold it back, easing pressure on the drainage system further downstream.

The colony of around eight is thriving and has welcomed at least two new kits to their family this year.
articles.reasoning: The reintroduction of beavers in west London has led to tangible environmental benefits by preventing flooding at Greenford station for three consecutive years. This demonstrates a successful nature-based solution with broad significance for urban flood management and ecosystem restoration. The article provides clear context and details about the impact and ongoing success of the project.
articles.personality_title: West London beavers help stop flooding at Greenford station
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – In west London, flooding has been a problem for Greenford station, especially during heavy rain. To help with this, beavers were brought back to a nearby area called Paradise Fields.

**What happened** – Since the beavers arrived about three years ago, Greenford station has not flooded. The beavers build dams and create wetlands, which slow down water flow and hold back rainwater. This helps reduce the pressure on the station’s drainage system.

**Impact** – This is important because it shows how animals and nature can help solve human problems like flooding. The beaver colony, about eight strong, is doing well and even had new baby beavers, called kits, this year. Their work protects the station and helps the environment.

**What's next step** – The project will continue to watch the beavers and their effects on flooding. If successful, this nature-based method might be used in other places to manage floods and improve urban areas.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Beavers in west London have naturally prevented flooding at Greenford station for three years by creating wetlands that slow rainwater flow.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les castors de l'ouest de Londres empêchent les inondations à la station Greenford
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Dans l'ouest de Londres, la station Greenford était souvent inondée lors de fortes pluies. Pour aider, des castors ont été réintroduits dans une zone proche appelée Paradise Fields.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Depuis l'arrivée des castors il y a environ trois ans, la station Greenford n'a plus été inondée. Les castors construisent des barrages et créent des zones humides, ce qui ralentit l'eau et retient la pluie. Cela réduit la pression sur le système de drainage de la station.

**Impact** – Cela montre que les animaux et la nature peuvent aider à résoudre des problèmes humains comme les inondations. La colonie d'environ huit castors se porte bien et a eu de nouveaux petits, appelés «kits», cette année. Leur travail protège la station et aide l'environnement.

**Prochaine étape** – Le projet continuera à observer les castors et leurs effets sur les inondations. Si cela marche, cette méthode naturelle pourrait être utilisée ailleurs pour gérer les inondations et améliorer les villes.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Les castors de l'ouest de Londres empêchent naturellement les inondations à la station Greenford depuis trois ans en créant des zones humides qui ralentissent l'eau de pluie.
articles.personality_title_es: Los castores del oeste de Londres evitan inundaciones en la estación Greenford
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – En el oeste de Londres, la estación Greenford sufría inundaciones durante lluvias fuertes. Para ayudar, se reintrodujeron castores en un área cercana llamada Paradise Fields.

**Qué pasó** – Desde que llegaron los castores hace unos tres años, la estación Greenford no se ha inundado. Los castores construyen presas y crean humedales que ralentizan el agua y retienen la lluvia. Esto reduce la presión en el sistema de drenaje de la estación.

**Impacto** – Esto muestra que los animales y la naturaleza pueden ayudar a resolver problemas humanos como las inundaciones. La colonia, de unas ocho castores, está creciendo y este año tuvieron nuevas crías, llamadas kits. Su trabajo protege la estación y ayuda al medio ambiente.

**Próximo paso** – El proyecto seguirá observando a los castores y cómo afectan las inundaciones. Si funciona bien, este método natural podría usarse en otros lugares para manejar las inundaciones y mejorar las ciudades.

**Resumen en una frase** – Los castores en el oeste de Londres han prevenido naturalmente las inundaciones en la estación Greenford durante tres años creando humedales que frenan el flujo de agua de lluvia.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a serene wetland scene at Paradise Fields with a family of beavers building dams and nurturing two playful kits near gently flowing water, while in the background a peaceful railway station stands dry and protected, all rendered in soft, natural earth tones and greens.

articles.title: Design for Disassembly: Breaking the Take-Make-Waste Product Cycle
articles.summary:
Nine percent. That’s the share of all plastic ever manufactured that has been recycled, according...
The post Design for Disassembly: Breaking the Take-Make-Waste Product Cycle appeared first on Earth911.
articles.content:
Nine percent. That’s the share of all plastic ever manufactured that has been recycled, according to a landmark 2017 analysis in Science Advances by University of California. In the years since that study, the rate hasn’t improved. Call it the 10% Rule: design a product with conventional methods today, and statistically, less than one unit in ten of whatever material you put into it will ever see a second life.

The 10% Rule isn’t a law of physics. It’s a design choice, embedded in every glued seam, every multi-material composite, every product shipped without a plan for what happens when it breaks. Design for Disassembly (DfD), which is based on a set of engineering principles that prioritize end-of-life separation from the earliest stages of product development, exists to break that pattern.

Understanding why the current system fails, and what a better-designed system looks like, is essential context for anyone who cares where products go when they leave the house.

The Built-In Disposal Problem

Most consumer products are composite objects made of multiple materials bonded, glued, welded, snapped, or co-molded in ways that make separation at end of life expensive, if not impossible.

A smartphone contains more than 60 elements from the periodic table, most present in quantities too small and too intermingled to recover economically under current conditions.

A standard mattress fuses polyurethane foam, steel springs, fabric, and chemical flame retardants into a single unit that costs more to disassemble than the recovered materials are worth.

A drip coffee maker is made with polypropylene, ABS plastic, stainless steel, copper wiring, and glass permanently joined by ultrasonic welding.

The result is what the EPA tracks as municipal solid waste, an enormous stream of recoverable value rendered unrecoverable by design. The US overall recycling rate for municipal solid waste hovers around 32%, but that aggregate number flatters the reality for individual product categories. Electronics recycling runs at roughly 17% globally, according to the UNEP Global E-Waste Monitor. Plastics, as the numbers show, fare worse. The 10% Rule describes the floor, the common state of recyclability, it’s not an outlier.

The core problem isn’t contamination in the recycling bin, though that matters. It’s that the products never had a path back. Material recovery was simply not a design requirement.

What Design for Disassembly Actually Means

DfD treats end-of-life separation as a first-order requirement alongside cost, performance, and the aesthetics of a product design. The goal is that every material in a product should be identifiable, accessible, and separable without destroying the value of adjacent components in the process.

In practice, DfD shows up as a cluster of choices that seem small in isolation but have significant over the life of a product and across the economy:

using a single material per component where possible;

specifying mechanical fasteners instead of adhesives;

selecting snap fits or standard screws instead of welded joints;

molding material identification codes directly into plastic parts; and

building modular architectures that allow component-level replacement rather than whole-unit disposal.

The Ellen MacArthur Foundation’s circular economy framework identifies DfD as a foundational strategy for keeping products and materials in productive use at their highest value.

DfD also requires a business model decision. The design only delivers its full value when the manufacturer has a reason to want the product back, whether through an extended producer responsibility obligation, a take-back program, or a remanufacturing operation that makes recovered materials economically worth pursuing.

Design, collection, and economics have to work together to make circularity profitable.

Where It Works: Examples From Practice

Several companies have demonstrated that DfD at consumer scale is achievable, not aspirational.

Fairphone, the Dutch electronics company, designs its smartphones so that owners can replace the battery, screen, and camera module using common screwdrivers. iFixit scores repairability on a 10-point scale; Fairphone consistently lands near the top of that scale, most recently with a “10” for its Fairphone 6. The commercial case is easy to understand: a phone that lasts longer keeps a customer in the ecosystem and keeps high-value materials out of the waste stream.

Caterpillar’s remanufacturing program, one of the largest industrial DfD operations in existence, takes back used engines, hydraulic components, and transmissions and restores them to original specification for resale. The program dramatically reduces both energy consumption and raw material demand compared to new manufacturing.

Herman Miller’s Aeron chair was designed with material identification on every component, accessible disassembly paths, and a take-back program. The company reports that a high percentage of the chair by weight is recyclable, a figure only achievable because the design makes separation feasible.

These aren’t niche experiments. They’re proof points that the 10% floor isn’t inherent to manufacturing, it’s inherent to manufacturing in the absence of DfD practices.

The Household Math

What does the 10% Rule cost a typical household, in concrete terms? Americans discard tens of millions of smartphones each year, each containing small but non-trivial quantities of gold, palladium, cobalt, and rare earth elements whose recovery depends entirely on design that enables separation. Those materials have real economic value, but they’re recoverable only if the product was built to release them.

The same logic applies to appliances, power tools, mattresses, and furniture, the durable goods that represent the largest share of household material consumption. When a washing machine goes to landfill because repair parts aren’t available and the design doesn’t support disassembly, the steel, copper, and polymers it contains leave the productive economy permanently.

The financial impact to households is diffuse but real: the inability to repair compresses replacement cycles and raises lifetime spending on goods. A phone designed for a $50 battery replacement versus a $600 replacement device is a direct household economic variable, not just an environmental one. The U.S. PIRG Education Fund has estimated that right-to-repair policies could save consumers as much as $49.6 billion annually in reduced replacement costs.

The Upstream Argument

DfD isn’t only a waste story, it’s an virgin materials extraction story, too. Every ton of aluminum recovered through disassembly is a ton that doesn’t require mining bauxite. Aluminum recycling uses approximately 95% less energy than primary production, according to The Aluminum Association. Steel recovery saves more than 60% of the energy required to produce steel from iron ore, according to the World Steel Association. Copper recovery runs similarly favorable. These are established figures that make recycled-material markets economically competitive when the supply is clean and separated, exactly what DfD enables.

The design choices made by product engineers in development stages determine whether those upstream savings are even possible downstream. DfD moves the intervention point to where it belongs: before the product is built, not after it breaks.

What You Can Do

Individual actions:

Choose repairable products. iFixit publishes repairability scores for electronics; treat them like a mileage rating when buying a car.

Use manufacturer take-back programs. Apple, Dell, and Best Buy all operate take-back and recycling programs. The product goes to a recovery stream designed for it.

Repair before you replace. An appliance repair extends material life by years. Earth911’s recycling search can help locate repair services alongside drop-off options for hard-to-recycle items.

Buy modular where possible; user-replaceable batteries, accessible components, standard fasteners. These are features that define a well-made product, not accidents.

Community and policy levers:

Support extended producer responsibility (EPR) legislation in your state. EPR laws require manufacturers to fund end-of-life management for their products; it’s the policy mechanism that makes DfD economically rational for companies that wouldn’t choose it voluntarily.

Back right-to-repair bills. The FTC’s 2021 report to Congress on right to repair documented the consumer and environmental case. Legislation requiring manufacturers to make parts and repair documentation available has passed in several states; more is pending at both state and federal levels.

Ask retailers about take-back when you purchase durable goods. Market signals from buyers change procurement decisions over time.

Related Reading
articles.reasoning: The article highlights Design for Disassembly (DfD) as a practical and scalable approach to significantly improve recycling rates and reduce waste by enabling easier product disassembly and material recovery. It presents concrete examples of companies implementing DfD, explains its broad environmental and economic benefits, and discusses policy and consumer actions to support circular economy principles. This represents a meaningful advancement in sustainable product design with large-scale positive impact potential.
articles.personality_title: Design for disassembly helps recycle more and reduce waste
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Most products today are made with many materials glued or welded together, making them hard to take apart and recycle. Because of this, only about 9% of all plastic ever made has been recycled. This problem happens because products are not designed to be taken apart easily when they are no longer useful.

**What happened** – Design for Disassembly (DfD) is a new way of making products so they can be taken apart easily at the end of their life. This means using screws instead of glue, single materials in parts, and making pieces replaceable. Some companies like Fairphone, Caterpillar, and Herman Miller are already using DfD to make phones, engines, and chairs that can be repaired, reused, or recycled more effectively.

**Impact** – DfD helps keep valuable materials like metals and plastics out of landfills, saving energy and reducing the need to mine new resources. It also saves money for consumers because products last longer and are easier to fix. For example, a phone designed for easy battery replacement costs less over time. This design approach challenges the usual “take-make-waste” cycle by making products part of a circular economy where materials are reused.

**What's next step** – More companies can adopt DfD, and laws like extended producer responsibility and right-to-repair can encourage this change. Consumers can support the shift by choosing repairable products, using take-back programs, and asking for products designed to be fixed or recycled. Together, these actions can make recycling more common and reduce waste worldwide.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Designing products to be easily taken apart helps recycle more materials, saves energy, and lowers costs by breaking the cycle of waste.
articles.personality_title_fr: La conception pour le démontage facilite le recyclage et réduit les déchets
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La plupart des produits sont aujourd’hui fabriqués avec plusieurs matériaux collés ou soudés, ce qui rend leur démontage et recyclage difficiles. À cause de cela, seulement environ 9 % de tout le plastique produit a été recyclé. Ce problème vient du fait que les produits ne sont pas conçus pour être facilement démontés en fin de vie.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – La conception pour le démontage (DfD) est une nouvelle méthode qui permet de fabriquer des produits pouvant être démontés facilement à la fin de leur utilisation. Cela signifie utiliser des vis au lieu de colle, des matériaux uniques pour chaque pièce, et rendre les composants remplaçables. Des entreprises comme Fairphone, Caterpillar et Herman Miller utilisent déjà la DfD pour fabriquer des téléphones, moteurs et chaises réparables, réutilisables ou recyclables.

**Impact** – La DfD aide à garder les matériaux précieux comme les métaux et plastiques hors des décharges, ce qui économise de l’énergie et réduit le besoin d’extraire de nouvelles ressources. Cela permet aussi aux consommateurs d’économiser de l’argent car les produits durent plus longtemps et sont plus faciles à réparer. Par exemple, un téléphone conçu pour changer facilement la batterie coûte moins cher sur le long terme. Cette approche remet en cause le cycle habituel « prendre-fabriquer-jeter » en intégrant les matériaux dans une économie circulaire.

**Prochaine étape** – Davantage d’entreprises peuvent adopter la DfD, et des lois comme la responsabilité élargie des producteurs et le droit à la réparation peuvent encourager ce changement. Les consommateurs peuvent soutenir cette évolution en choisissant des produits réparables, en utilisant les programmes de reprise, et en demandant des produits conçus pour être réparés ou recyclés. Ensemble, ces actions peuvent rendre le recyclage plus courant et réduire les déchets dans le monde.

**Phrase à retenir** – Concevoir des produits faciles à démonter permet de recycler plus de matériaux, d’économiser de l’énergie et de réduire les coûts en brisant le cycle des déchets.
articles.personality_title_es: El diseño para el desmontaje facilita el reciclaje y reduce los residuos
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La mayoría de los productos están hechos hoy con muchos materiales pegados o soldados, lo que dificulta desmontarlos y reciclarlos. Por eso, solo alrededor del 9 % de todo el plástico fabricado se ha reciclado. Este problema ocurre porque los productos no están diseñados para ser fácilmente desmontados cuando ya no se usan.

**Qué pasó** – El Diseño para el Desmontaje (DfD) es una forma nueva de fabricar productos para que se puedan desmontar fácilmente al final de su vida útil. Esto incluye usar tornillos en lugar de pegamento, materiales únicos por pieza y hacer las partes reemplazables. Algunas empresas como Fairphone, Caterpillar y Herman Miller ya usan DfD para crear teléfonos, motores y sillas que se pueden reparar, reutilizar o reciclar mejor.

**Impacto** – El DfD ayuda a mantener materiales valiosos como metales y plásticos fuera de los vertederos, ahorrando energía y reduciendo la necesidad de extraer recursos nuevos. También ahorra dinero a los consumidores porque los productos duran más y son más fáciles de arreglar. Por ejemplo, un teléfono diseñado para cambiar fácilmente la batería cuesta menos con el tiempo. Este enfoque rompe el ciclo habitual de “tomar-hacer-desechar” al integrar los materiales en una economía circular.

**Próximo paso** – Más empresas pueden adoptar DfD, y leyes como la responsabilidad extendida del productor y el derecho a reparar pueden impulsar este cambio. Los consumidores pueden apoyar el cambio eligiendo productos reparables, usando programas de devolución y pidiendo productos diseñados para ser reparados o reciclados. Estas acciones pueden hacer que el reciclaje sea más común y reducir los residuos en todo el mundo.

**Frase clave** – Diseñar productos para que sean fáciles de desmontar ayuda a reciclar más materiales, ahorrar energía y reducir costos al romper el ciclo de desperdicio.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a partially disassembled smartphone and modular furniture pieces resting on a wooden workbench bathed in soft natural light, with visible screws, snap-fit joints, and separated material layers gently arranged to symbolize thoughtful design for easy repair and recycling.

articles.title: Urban trees aren’t just nice, scientists say — they’re mandatory
articles.content:
They tower overhead and sway in the wind and often teem with squawking birds, yet trees are easy to ignore. Urbanites rush by them without noticing, and without appreciating all the work they do: Trees reduce temperatures, mitigate flooding, and provide habitat for animals.

City leaders are no exception to this oversight. As mayors around the world pledge to reduce municipal greenhouse gas emissions, they’re missing the literal low-hanging fruit of bolstering urban forests, dozens of scientists argue in a new essay. “We have to elevate it from something that is nice to have to something that we require — like, mandatory,” said Manuel Esperon-Rodriguez, an ecologist at Bangor University in the United Kingdom and lead author of the piece, which published today in the journal PLOS Climate. “In the same way that we treat education, security, transportation, it has to be elevated to that level.”

What makes urban forestry so important? For one, trees significantly cool the concrete jungle by providing shade and releasing water vapor to “sweat.” Patches of greenery also allow stormwater to soak into the ground instead of pooling and flooding — that investment alone will spare cities from economic damages as a warming atmosphere makes rain fall harder. Spending time in parks also boosts mental health, while urban farms produce nutritious food and create jobs. Planting trees, especially native species, also provides shelter and food for fauna. At the same time, vegetation absorbs pollutants, improving air quality for everyone.

These scientists have laid out a four-point approach to funding, raising, and maintaining urban forests. This, by the way, includes individual trees on sidewalks, parks, and woodlands in cities. But it’s really about all the vegetation — not just trees but shrubs as well — within the city limits, whether that’s in someone’s backyard or growing in a street median.

To support our nonprofit environmental journalism, please consider disabling your ad-blocker to allow ads on Grist. Here's How

The first hurdle is investing in this stuff. Urban forestry isn’t just about buying a bunch of trees and hiring people to put them in the ground. It takes resources to maintain them, especially when they’re newly planted and not yet established, and therefore more vulnerable to stresses like pests. Money can (and does) come from private funders, but that cash isn’t always a guarantee. So city governments should be setting aside money for these green spaces, the researchers argue. “We say that it has to be critical infrastructure, because then we need a special budget dedicated just to them,” Esperon-Rodriguez said.

Even for cash-strapped governments, this is an investment proven to bring dividends: A recent report found that for every dollar put into parks and recreation, cities reap $3 in local economic benefits every year. That’s because green spaces encourage people to exercise, supporting public health and reducing the costs associated with sedentary lifestyles. By attracting locals and tourists, parks also spur economic activity as folks filter into surrounding neighborhoods to shop or have lunch. So while yes, it does take money to plant and maintain this greenery, it’s in a city’s best interests to do so.

Mayors must ensure that these domains blossom in an equitable way, the scientists add. Richer areas tend to be much greener, and therefore cooler, than underserved neighborhoods. People who can’t afford air conditioning are at higher risk of the urban heat island effect, or the tendency for the built environment to absorb the sun’s energy all day and release it throughout the night. “Then what’s the cost?” Esperon-Rodriguez asked. “They are missing opportunities, they are missing recreational activities. And if they don’t have air conditioning, then on top of that there is the issue of health.”

Officials can’t just roll into a neighborhood and plant trees, though — the essay argues that cities have to collaborate with their communities on strategies for doing so. Some folks might want more fruit trees, for instance, while others might object to cherries splatting on the sidewalk. Some might worry about their allergies, and request trees that don’t spew so much pollen.

To support our nonprofit environmental journalism, please consider disabling your ad-blocker to allow ads on Grist. Here's How

Esperon-Rodriguez adds that expanding the canopy across a metropolis, and doing so equitably, needs to be enshrined in some way. That is, it can’t just be a mayoral candidate’s promise to increase tree cover by 30 percent, but something that’s legislated. This is not only more durable over the years, and hopefully decades, but helps citizens hold elected officials accountable if they’re not meeting targets, Esperon-Rodriguez said.

Overall, these campaigns need to be evidence-based, the essay argues. Cities, for example, have to identify not just the tree species that communities prefer, but ones that will actually survive ever-climbing temperatures. It’s not just thinking about increasing the canopy in the near term to meet some goal, but making sure cities are more verdant and safer in the long run. “It’s a way to secure,” Esperon-Rodriguez said, “that whatever we’re planting today is going to survive the next 10, 20, or 50 years.”
articles.reasoning: The article highlights the critical role of urban trees in mitigating climate impacts, improving public health, and promoting economic benefits. It calls for city-wide, equitable, and legislated urban forestry initiatives, showing a meaningful and broad positive impact on society and the environment with detailed context and actionable recommendations.
articles.personality_title: Scientists call for making urban trees a required part of city planning
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Trees in cities often go unnoticed, but they play important roles like cooling down neighborhoods, preventing floods, and providing homes for animals. Despite these benefits, many city leaders have not made urban forests a top priority.

**What happened** – A group of scientists published an essay urging city leaders worldwide to treat urban trees as essential infrastructure, just like schools or transportation. They explained that trees help cool cities, soak up rainwater, improve air quality, and support mental health. The scientists suggest cities need dedicated budgets and plans to plant and maintain trees fairly across all neighborhoods.

**Impact** – This call is important because it shifts the view of trees from something nice to something necessary. Trees can help reduce heat in poorer neighborhoods that often lack air conditioning and green spaces. Investing in trees also benefits local economies by encouraging people to spend time outside and shop nearby. The scientists emphasize that planting trees should be done with input from local communities to meet their needs.

**What's next step** – Cities will need to create laws and budgets to protect and grow urban forests, making sure tree planting efforts last for decades. They must choose tree types that can survive hotter temperatures and work closely with residents to decide what kinds of trees to plant.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists want cities to make planting and caring for urban trees a required, long-term part of city planning to improve health, climate resilience, and fairness.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques demandent de rendre les arbres urbains indispensables dans les villes
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les arbres en ville sont souvent ignorés, pourtant ils jouent un rôle important comme rafraîchir l’air, prévenir les inondations et offrir un habitat aux animaux. Malgré ces avantages, beaucoup de responsables urbains ne les considèrent pas comme une priorité.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Un groupe de scientifiques a publié un essai demandant aux dirigeants des villes du monde entier de considérer les arbres urbains comme une infrastructure essentielle, au même titre que les écoles ou les transports. Ils expliquent que les arbres rafraîchissent les villes, absorbent l’eau de pluie, améliorent la qualité de l’air et soutiennent la santé mentale. Les scientifiques proposent que les villes disposent de budgets spécifiques et de plans pour planter et entretenir les arbres équitablement dans tous les quartiers.

**Impact** – Cet appel est important car il change la vision des arbres, passant d’un simple « plus » à une nécessité. Les arbres peuvent réduire la chaleur dans les quartiers pauvres souvent dépourvus de climatisation et d’espaces verts. Investir dans les arbres profite aussi à l’économie locale en encourageant les habitants à sortir et à consommer dans les commerces proches. Les scientifiques insistent sur la nécessité de planter les arbres en concertation avec les habitants pour répondre à leurs besoins.

**Prochaine étape** – Les villes devront créer des lois et des budgets pour protéger et développer les forêts urbaines, en veillant à ce que ces efforts durent des décennies. Elles devront choisir des espèces capables de supporter la chaleur et travailler avec les résidents pour décider quels arbres planter.

**Conclusion en une phrase** – Les scientifiques souhaitent que les villes rendent la plantation et l’entretien des arbres urbains obligatoires et durables pour améliorer la santé, la résistance au climat et l’équité.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos piden que los árboles urbanos sean obligatorios en las ciudades
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los árboles en las ciudades a menudo pasan desapercibidos, pero cumplen funciones importantes como enfriar los barrios, evitar inundaciones y proporcionar hogar a animales. A pesar de estos beneficios, muchos líderes urbanos no los han priorizado.

**Qué sucedió** – Un grupo de científicos publicó un ensayo pidiendo a los líderes de ciudades en todo el mundo que consideren los árboles urbanos como infraestructura esencial, al igual que las escuelas o el transporte. Explican que los árboles ayudan a enfriar las ciudades, absorber el agua de lluvia, mejorar la calidad del aire y apoyar la salud mental. Los científicos sugieren que las ciudades deben contar con presupuestos y planes dedicados para plantar y cuidar los árboles de forma equitativa en todos los barrios.

**Impacto** – Esta petición es importante porque cambia la forma de ver los árboles, de algo agradable a algo necesario. Los árboles pueden ayudar a reducir el calor en barrios pobres que a menudo carecen de aire acondicionado y espacios verdes. Invertir en árboles también beneficia la economía local al animar a las personas a salir y comprar cerca. Los científicos destacan que la plantación debe hacerse con la participación de las comunidades para satisfacer sus necesidades.

**Próximo paso** – Las ciudades deberán crear leyes y presupuestos para proteger y expandir los bosques urbanos, asegurando que estos esfuerzos duren décadas. Deben elegir especies que resistan temperaturas más altas y trabajar con los vecinos para decidir qué árboles plantar.

**Frase clave** – Los científicos quieren que las ciudades hagan obligatoria y duradera la plantación y cuidado de árboles urbanos para mejorar la salud, la resistencia climática y la equidad.
articles.image_prompt: A vibrant cityscape where towering, flourishing native trees with lush canopies intertwine with diverse urban elements—sidewalks, parks, and street medians—while colorful birds nest and flutter among the branches, all bathed in warm, natural tones that evoke harmony between urban life and carefully nurtured green spaces fostering community well-being and environmental resilience.

articles.title: Fixing fast fashion: France will pay you to repair your clothes
articles.summary: Your ripped clothes deserve better than the bottom of your closet or – worse – the trash can. The French government is now paying people to get them repaired, in a bid to extend the life of clothing and avoid waste. Under the Repair Bonus scheme, consumers can get a discount worth up to €25 per item. Since its launch in 2023, more than 1 million repairs have been subsidised. Our Down to Earth team reports.
articles.content:
Fixing fast fashion: France will pay you to repair your clothes

France

To display this content from YouTube, you must enable advertisement tracking and audience measurement. Accept Manage my choices One of your browser extensions seems to be blocking the video player from loading. To watch this content, you may need to disable it on this site. Try again Cover image: An estimated 700,000 tonnes of clothing is thrown away in France every single year. © FRANCE 24 04:55

04:55 min Share

From the show

Reading time 1 min

Your ripped clothes deserve better than the bottom of your closet or – worse – the trash can. The French government is now paying people to get them repaired, in a bid to extend the life of clothing and avoid waste. Under the Repair Bonus scheme, consumers can get a discount worth up to €25 per item. Since its launch in 2023, more than 1 million repairs have been subsidised. Our Down to Earth team reports.
articles.reasoning: The article describes a government policy in France that financially incentivizes clothing repairs to reduce textile waste, which has a broad environmental impact by promoting sustainability and reducing landfill waste. The initiative has already subsidized over 1 million repairs, showing significant scale and tangible positive environmental effects.
articles.personality_title: France pays people to fix clothes and reduce waste
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Every year, people in France throw away around 700,000 tonnes of clothing. This waste adds up in landfills and harms the environment. Many clothes are thrown away even if they can still be worn with a simple repair.

**What happened** – In 2023, the French government started the Repair Bonus scheme. This program gives people up to €25 off for each clothing repair. The goal is to encourage people to fix their clothes instead of throwing them away. So far, over 1 million repairs have been supported through this program.

**Impact** – This program helps reduce the huge amount of clothes waste in France by making repairs cheaper and easier. It also helps people keep their favorite clothes longer, which saves money and cuts down on pollution from making new clothes. The large number of repairs shows many people are choosing to fix clothes rather than discard them.

**What's next step** – The government plans to continue supporting the Repair Bonus and may expand it to cover more types of clothing or increase the discount. Other countries might look at this program as an example to reduce clothing waste and protect the environment.

**One-sentence takeaway** – France’s Repair Bonus encourages people to fix clothes by offering discounts, helping reduce waste and extend the life of garments.
articles.personality_title_fr: La France vous paie pour réparer vos vêtements et réduire les déchets
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Chaque année, environ 700 000 tonnes de vêtements sont jetées en France. Ces déchets remplissent les décharges et polluent l’environnement. Beaucoup de vêtements sont jetés alors qu’ils pourraient être réparés.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – En 2023, le gouvernement français a lancé le dispositif « Bonus Réparation ». Ce programme offre jusqu’à 25 € de réduction pour chaque réparation de vêtement. L’objectif est d’encourager les gens à réparer leurs habits plutôt que de les jeter. Plus d’un million de réparations ont déjà été aidées grâce à ce programme.

**Impact** – Ce programme aide à réduire la grande quantité de vêtements jetés en France en rendant les réparations moins chères et plus accessibles. Il permet aussi aux gens de garder leurs vêtements préférés plus longtemps, ce qui économise de l’argent et réduit la pollution liée à la fabrication de nouveaux habits. Le nombre important de réparations montre que beaucoup choisissent de réparer.

**Prochaine étape** – Le gouvernement prévoit de continuer à soutenir le Bonus Réparation et pourrait l’étendre à plus de types de vêtements ou augmenter la réduction. D’autres pays pourraient s’inspirer de ce programme pour réduire leurs déchets textiles.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Le Bonus Réparation en France encourage la réparation des vêtements grâce à des réductions, ce qui aide à diminuer les déchets et à prolonger la durée de vie des habits.
articles.personality_title_es: Francia paga a las personas para reparar su ropa y reducir residuos
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Cada año, en Francia se tiran alrededor de 700 000 toneladas de ropa. Estos residuos llenan los vertederos y dañan el medio ambiente. Muchas prendas se tiran aunque podrían arreglarse fácilmente.

**Qué pasó** – En 2023, el gobierno francés lanzó el programa Bono de Reparación. Este programa ofrece hasta 25 € de descuento por cada reparación de ropa. El objetivo es que las personas arreglen su ropa en lugar de tirarla. Hasta ahora, más de un millón de reparaciones han recibido apoyo.

**Impacto** – Este programa ayuda a reducir la gran cantidad de ropa que se tira en Francia al hacer que las reparaciones sean más baratas y accesibles. También ayuda a que la gente conserve sus prendas favoritas por más tiempo, lo que ahorra dinero y reduce la contaminación causada por fabricar ropa nueva. El gran número de reparaciones muestra que muchas personas prefieren arreglar.

**Próximo paso** – El gobierno planea seguir apoyando el Bono de Reparación y podría ampliarlo para cubrir más tipos de ropa o aumentar el descuento. Otros países podrían seguir este ejemplo para reducir los residuos textiles.

**Conclusión en una frase** – El Bono de Reparación en Francia incentiva a reparar la ropa con descuentos, ayudando a reducir residuos y alargar la vida útil de las prendas.
articles.image_prompt: A cozy, softly lit tailor’s workspace with a vintage sewing machine stitching a colorful patch onto a worn garment, surrounded by neatly folded repaired clothes and gentle hands mending fabric, all rendered in warm, natural tones with a textured, painterly style.
